explicit_text: I was born and raised in Richmond County, South Carolina, just two years after freedom. I stayed there until 1911, working on farms and doing odd jobs. My parents, Steven and Susan Scott, were former slaves, and I grew up in a family of five. Richmond County was where my early life and struggles began before I moved to Florida.

explicit_text: I lived in South Carolina until 1911, where I farmed and worked various jobs as a young man. It was there that I first voted, but after moving to Florida, I never voted again due to racial discrimination. South Carolina was my home during my early years, shaped by my family's history and the legacy of slavery.

explicit_text: I came to Florida in 1911 as a grown man and worked in turpentine stills moving from place to place. I lived in several towns like Homosassa, Citrus County, and later moved to my current home in 1926. Florida is where I built my farm, raised my family, and became involved in church activities as a Baptist pastor.

explicit_text: I enlisted as a soldier in the Spanish–American War when I was thirty years old at Suwanneeville, North Carolina. I was part of Major Young's Company, and we traveled from Savannah, Georgia, heading to Cuba. However, the war ended before we landed, so we returned to Savannah and were mustered out. It was a time of excitement and trouble, but we all went home singing and shouting.
